preFix.title: Heat batteries as an alternative to electrochemical battery storage
preFix.subtitle: How thermal stores use cheap electricity to reduce your heating bill
preFix.excerpt: A heat battery stores cheap off-peak or excess solar electricity as heat in a high-temperature ceramic or phase-change material core. Later, it releases that heat through underfloor circuits, radiators, or a heat exchanger. Capital cost is lower than lithium-ion storage and there is no battery-cycle degradation. For a house with solar and a high space-heating or hot-water demand, a heat battery can be more cost-effective than a chemical battery pack.
preFix.sourceNotes: Tepeo ZEB heat battery technical specification 2024. Sunamp UniQ PCM thermal store data sheets. Emersion Technologies heat battery trial results 2023. Solar Trade Association thermal storage guide 2024.

content.text: A lithium-ion home battery stores electricity and gives it back as electricity. A heat battery converts electricity to heat and stores that heat for central heating or hot water. For homes where most energy use is for heating rather than appliances. A heat battery can give more thermal output per pound of investment.
The two main commercial approaches differ in the storage medium:.

Ceramic brick core: high-density ceramic bricks heated to 700 to 900°C by embedded electric elements during cheap-rate hours. Heat is discharged by forcing air or water through the core. Controlled by the home's thermostat or a smart interface. Storage capacity: 7 to 20 kWh thermal per unit.

Phase-change material: the PCM melts at 58°C to 90°C during charging and solidifies during discharge. Releasing heat at near-constant temperature. The compact size suits houses without the space for a traditional hot water cylinder. Storage capacity: 5 to 24 kWh thermal per unit.

A lithium-ion battery stores electricity and can power any appliance in the home. A heat battery stores heat and can only serve space heating and hot water. The cost per kWh of storage capacity is 30 to 50% lower for a heat battery. But the application is narrower.
When a heat battery makes sense
You have solar PV and most of your daytime export goes to the grid unused. Either your roof is too small for a diverter. Or the immersion diverter is already using all the surplus.
Your tariff includes cheap overnight rates. Your home uses a lot of heat: for example, a semi or large house with poor insulation.
You are replacing a gas boiler and want all-electric heat without fitting a heat pump or changing the piping system.

A 1970s detached house in Reading, all-electric, 15,000 kWh/year space and water heating demand. Switching from single-rate to an off-peak tariff saves substantially on energy bills when all heating is shifted overnight. The heat battery stores 12 kWh of off-peak electricity; the heating runs from stored heat during peak hours. Payback is 7 to 10 years depending on the tariff spread.
Step-by-step instructions for Heat batteries as an alternative to electrochemical battery storage go here.
